questions.question: What is the default OSPF hello interval on a broadcast network?
questions.explanation: OSPF sends hellos every 10 seconds on broadcast and point-to-point networks; dead interval is 4x = 40 seconds.

questions.question: What is the correct order of OSPF neighbor states?
options: Down → Init → 2-Way → ExStart → Exchange → Loading → Full
Down → Init → ExStart → 2-Way → Loading → Exchange → Full
Init → Down → 2-Way → Exchange → ExStart → Loading → Full
Down → 2-Way → Init → ExStart → Loading → Exchange → Full
questions.explanation: The OSPF neighbor state progression is Down → Init → 2-Way → ExStart → Exchange → Loading → Full. DR/BDR is elected at 2-Way; LSDBs sync during Exchange and Loading.

questions.question: Which OSPF LSA type is generated by every OSPF router to describe its interfaces?
questions.explanation: Type 1 Router LSAs are generated by every OSPF router and list that router's OSPF-activated interfaces and networks.

questions.question: Which OSPF LSA type is generated only by the DR on a multi-access network?
questions.explanation: The DR generates Type 2 Network LSAs on multi-access networks, listing all routers attached to the segment.

questions.question: An ASBR advertises a default route into OSPF. Which LSA type carries this?
questions.explanation: Type 5 AS-External LSAs are generated by ASBRs to advertise external routes (routes outside the OSPF domain) such as a default route injected via default-information originate.

questions.question: What is the OSPF cost formula?
questions.explanation: OSPF cost = reference bandwidth ÷ interface bandwidth. Default reference bandwidth is 100 Mbps. Values less than 1 round up to 1.

questions.question: Why is it recommended to raise the OSPF reference bandwidth from the default?
options: To reduce CPU load on routers
Because the default of 100 Mbps makes FastEth, GigE, and 10GigE all cost 1 — hiding faster-link preference
questions.explanation: With the default 100 Mbps reference, any link 100 Mbps or faster has cost 1 — the router can't distinguish GigE from 10GigE. Raising it (e.g., `auto-cost reference-bandwidth 100000`) differentiates them.

questions.question: In DR/BDR election, what is the default OSPF interface priority, and what value means the router can never become DR?
questions.explanation: Default OSPF priority is 1. Setting it to 0 means the router will never become DR or BDR. Max is 255 (highest priority wins).

questions.question: If OSPF priority is tied during DR election, what breaks the tie?
questions.explanation: On a priority tie, the highest router ID wins the DR election. DR/BDR is non-preemptive — once elected, a new router with a higher RID won't displace the current DR.

questions.question: What multicast address does OSPF use for all OSPF routers?
questions.explanation: All-OSPF-routers multicast is 224.0.0.5. 224.0.0.6 is all-DR/BDR routers. OSPF uses IP protocol number 89.

questions.question: What is the router ID selection priority in OSPF?
options: Highest physical interface IP → highest loopback IP → manual router-id
Manual router-id → highest loopback IP → highest physical interface IP
Lowest loopback IP → manual router-id → highest physical interface IP
questions.explanation: OSPF selects the RID using: manual `router-id` command first, then highest loopback IP, then highest active physical interface IP. Manual is always preferred.

questions.question: Which OSPF neighbor requirement, if mismatched, will cause neighbors to stall in the ExStart state?
questions.explanation: Mismatched MTU causes OSPF neighbors to get stuck in ExStart. Hello/dead mismatches prevent neighbors from forming at all; area and authentication mismatches prevent adjacency earlier.

questions.question: On a broadcast segment, what state do two DROthers remain in with each other?
questions.explanation: DROthers stay in 2-Way state with other DROthers — they don't form full adjacencies. Each DROther forms Full adjacency only with the DR and BDR.

questions.question: What happens in OSPF's Exchange state?
options: Hellos are exchanged to discover neighbors
DBDs (Database Descriptions) are exchanged to describe each LSDB
LSUs carrying actual LSAs are transmitted
questions.explanation: In Exchange state, neighbors exchange DBD packets summarizing their LSDBs. Loading state then uses LSR/LSU/LSAck to request and deliver missing LSAs.

questions.question: What is the default OSPF dead interval on a non-broadcast network?
questions.explanation: Non-broadcast (e.g., Frame Relay) defaults are Hello 30 s, Dead 120 s — 4x the hello timer. Broadcast and point-to-point use Hello 10 s, Dead 40 s.
